strict.definition: Having very firm rules that must be followed.
strict.example: Some parents are very strict about bedtime.

approaches.definition: Different ways of dealing with something.
approaches.example: There are three main approaches to child discipline.

steer.definition: To guide or direct someone toward a certain way.
steer.example: Parents try to steer their child's behaviour.

discipline.definition: Training people to obey rules or behave well.
discipline.example: Discipline should be done with kindness.

imposing.definition: Forcing something on someone without their choice.
imposing.example: Imposing your will on a child can cause problems.

insist.definition: To demand strongly that something must happen.
insist.example: She insisted that her child tidy the room.

tidies.definition: Makes a place clean and organized.
tidies.example: He tidies his room every weekend.

punish.definition: To make someone suffer for doing something wrong.
punish.example: Don't punish your child for having feelings.

compliant.definition: Willing to do what others ask without arguing.

stand-offs.definition: Situations where two sides refuse to give in.
stand-offs.example: Being too strict leads to stand-offs with your child.

humiliation.definition: A feeling of deep shame or embarrassment.
humiliation.example: Strict parenting can lead to humiliation and anger.

modelling.definition: Showing by example how to behave.
modelling.example: Parents are modelling behaviour for their children.

inflexible.definition: Not willing to change or adjust.
inflexible.example: Being inflexible teaches your child to be the same.

tolerance.definition: The ability to accept something difficult without getting upset.
tolerance.example: Children need to develop tolerance for frustration.

intolerant.definition: Not able to accept differences or difficulties.
intolerant.example: Strict parents may raise intolerant children.

inadvertently.example: You could inadvertently teach your child to be stubborn.

reciprocal.definition: Done in return; going both ways.
reciprocal.example: They got into a cycle of reciprocal inflexibility.

withdrawal.definition: Pulling away from someone or stopping communication.
withdrawal.example: The child's withdrawal from talking was a warning sign.

authoritarian.definition: Ruling with strict control and demanding obedience.
authoritarian.example: An authoritarian parent does not listen to the child's feelings.

cooperate.example: Children learn to cooperate when they feel heard.

authority.definition: The power or right to give orders and make decisions.
authority.example: Children need a healthy relationship with authority.

dictator.definition: A person who rules with total power and no one can disagree.
dictator.example: You should be a counsellor, not a dictator.

morality.definition: Ideas about what is right and wrong.
morality.example: Good parenting nurtures morality in children.

lax.definition: Not strict enough; too relaxed about rules.
lax.example: Being lax means giving the child no boundaries.

boundaries.definition: Rules or limits that help people know what is acceptable.
boundaries.example: Children need boundaries to feel safe.

backlash.definition: A strong reaction against something that happened before.
backlash.example: Being too lax is sometimes a backlash against strict parents.

anxiety.definition: A feeling of worry and nervousness.
anxiety.example: Anxiety can make parents too strict or too lax.

risk-averse.definition: Wanting to avoid any danger or chance of something going wrong.
risk-averse.example: Risk-averse parents try to protect children from everything.

upbringing.definition: The way a child is raised and taught by their parents.
upbringing.example: His strict upbringing affected how he parents his own children.

establish.definition: To set up or create something that lasts.
establish.example: Some children can establish their own standards.

overwhelmed.definition: Feeling that there is too much to handle.
overwhelmed.example: The child felt overwhelmed by the messy room.

erosion.definition: A slow wearing away or loss of something.
erosion.example: Fighting causes an erosion of goodwill between you.

goodwill.definition: Friendly and helpful feelings toward someone.
goodwill.example: Battles can lead to an erosion of goodwill.

intentionally.definition: Done on purpose, not by accident.
intentionally.example: She was intentionally choosing to wait until her child was ready.

collaborative.definition: Working together to solve a problem.
collaborative.example: The collaborative method involves solving problems together.

counsellor.definition: A person who gives advice and helps solve problems.
counsellor.example: Be more of a counsellor than a dictator with your child.

validate.definition: To show that someone's feelings are real and acceptable.
validate.example: Validate your child's feelings before solving the problem.

brainstorm.definition: To think of many ideas together to solve a problem.

tricky.definition: Difficult to deal with, needing careful handling.
tricky.example: Stage 2 can be tricky for parents.

endorse.definition: To support or approve of something.
endorse.example: It can feel hard to say something you don't want to endorse.

heels.definition: In 'dig in their heels': to refuse to change or give up.
heels.example: Not acknowledging feelings makes a child dig in their heels.

multiple-choice.definition: Offering several options to choose from.
multiple-choice.example: Use a multiple-choice approach to find out feelings.

nailed down.definition: Figured out exactly; made clear and certain.
nailed down.example: Once you have nailed down their feelings, you can help.

redefine.definition: To describe or explain something in a new way.
redefine.example: You can redefine the problem once you know the feelings.

shame.definition: To make someone feel bad about who they are or what they did.
shame.example: Don't shame your child for having messy feelings.

threaten.definition: To warn that something bad will happen.
threaten.example: Threatening to throw away toys only builds resentment.

resentment.definition: A feeling of anger that lasts because of unfair treatment.
resentment.example: Threats build up resentment in children.

empathize.definition: To understand and share another person's feelings.
empathize.example: Children learn to empathize when their feelings are considered.

counter-intuitive.definition: The opposite of what you would naturally expect.
counter-intuitive.example: It feels counter-intuitive to accept a child's complaint.

dismiss.definition: To refuse to consider or pay attention to something.
dismiss.example: Don't dismiss your child's ideas during brainstorming.

disempower.definition: To take away someone's power or confidence.
disempower.example: Being the one with all the answers will disempower your child.

folding.definition: Bending clothes neatly so they can be put away.

long-winded.definition: Taking too long to say or do something.
long-winded.example: The collaborative method may seem long-winded.

compromise.definition: An agreement where each side gives up something.
compromise.example: Learning to compromise is an important life skill.

socialized.definition: Learned how to behave well with other people.
socialized.example: These skills are essential for socialized behaviour.

empathy.definition: The ability to understand how someone else feels.
empathy.example: Empathy is one of the most important skills to develop.
